title: 第 29 節
en: I believe that there can be little doubt that Aditi, the boundless, was one of the oldest names of the dawn, or more correctly, of that portion of the sky from whence every morning the light and life of the world flashed forth.

Look at the dawn, and forget for a moment your astronomy ; and I ask you whether, when the dark THE IDEAS OF INFINITY AND LAW. 229 veil of the night is slowly lifted, and the air becomes transparent and alive, and light streams forth, you know not whence, you would not feel that your eye, stretching as far as it can stretch, and yet stretching in vain, was looking into the very eye of the infinite 1 To the ancient seers the dawn seemed to open the golden gates of another world, and while these gates were open for the sun to pass in triumph, their eyes and their mind strove in their childish way to pierce beyond the limits of this finite world. The dawn came and went, but there remained always behind the dawn that heaving sea of light or fire from which she springs. Was not this the visible infinite ? And what better name could be given than that which the Vedie poets gave to it, Aditi, the boundless, the yonder, the beyond all and everything 1 Thus, I believe, we can understand how a deity, which at first seemed to us so abstract as to have no birthplace anywhere in nature, so modern that we could hardly believe in its occurrence in the Veda, may have been one of the earliest intuitions and creations of the Hindu mind1. In later times the boundless Aditi may have become identified with the sky, also with the earth, but originally she was far beyond the sky and the earth.

Thus we read in a hymn2 addressed to Mitra and Varu?za, representatives of day and night, ' 0 Mitra 1 I have treated fully of Aditi in the Rig- Veda, in my trans lation of the Rig- Veda Sanhita, vol. I, pp. 230-251. There is an excellent essay by Dr. Alfred Hillebrandt, ' Uber die Gottin Aditi/ 1876. He (p. n) derives the word from da, 'to bind,' but prefers to explain Aditi by imperishableness, and guards against the idea that Aditi could mean omnipresent.
2 Rig- Veda, V, 62, 8.

and Vartwa, you mount your chariot which, at the dawning of the dawn, is golden-coloured, and has iron poles at the setting of the sun l : from thence you see Aditi and Diti' — that is, what is yonder and what is here, what is infinite and what is finite, what is mortal and what is immortal 2.
Another poet speaks of the dawn as the face of Aditi3, thus indicating that Aditi is here not the dawn itself, but something beyond the dawn.

As the sun and all the solar deities rise from the east, we can well understand how Aditi came to be called the mother of the bright gods, and more particularly of Mitra and Varu^a (Rig- Veda, X, 36, 3), of Aryaman and Bhaga, and at last of the seven, or even eight so-called Adityas, that is, the solar deities, rising from the east. Surra, the sun, is called not only Aditya (Rig-Veda, VIII, 101, n, ba£ mahan asi surya, ba£ aditya mahan asi, * Truly, Surya, thou art great ; truly, Aditya, thou art great '); but also Aditeya (Rig- Veda, X, 88, u).

It was, no doubt, the frequent mention of these her sons that gave to Aditi almost from the beginning a decidedly feminine character. She is the mother, with powerful, terrible, with royal sons. But there are passages where Aditi seems to be conceived as a male deity, or anyhow as a sexless being.
Though Aditi is more closely connected with the dawn, yet she is soon invoked, not only in the morning, but likewise at noon, and in the evening 4.
1 The contrast between the light of the morning and the evening, seems expressed by the colour of the two metals, gold and iron. 2 Rig- Veda, I, 35, 2.
3 Ibid., I, 113, 19, dditer anikam. 4 Ibid., V, 69, 3.

THE IDEAS OF INFINITY AND LAW. 231 When we read in the Atharva-Veda, X, 8, 16 : ' That whence the sun rises, and that where he sets, that I believe is the oldest, and no one goes beyond,' we might almost translate ' the oldest ' by Aditi. Aditi soon receives her full share of veneration and worship, and she is implored, not only to drive away darkness and the enemies that lurk in the dark, but likewise to deliver man from any sin which he may have committed.
Darkness and sin.

These two ideas — darkness and sin — which seem to us far apart, are closely connected with each other in the minds of the early Aryans. I shall read you some extracts to show how often one idea, the fear of enemies, evokes the other, the fear of sin, or what we should call, our worst enemy. * 0 Adityas J, deliver us from the mouth of the wolves, like a bound thief, 0 Aditi ! ' ' May Aditi 2 by day protect our cattle, may she, who never deceives, protect by night ; may she, with steady increase, protect us from evil.' (Amhasa/^, literally, from anxiety, from choking produced by the consciousness of sin.) 'And may she, the wise Aditi, come with help to us by day ! may she kindly bring happiness, and drive away all enemies ! '

Or again 3 : ' Aditi, Mitra, and also Yaruwa, forgive, if we have committed any sin against you ! May I obtain the wide fearless light, 0 Indra ! May not the long darkness come over us ! ' ' May Aditi grant us sinlessness 4 ! '
One other idea seems very naturally to have 1 Kig-Yeda, VIII, 67, 14. 2 Ibid., VIII, 18, 6, 7.

sprung up from the concept of Aditi. Wherever we go, we find that one of the earliest imaginings of a future life arose from the contemplation of the daily coming and going of the sun and other heavenly bodies 1. As we still say, ' his sun has set/ they said and believed that those who departed this life would go to the west, to the setting of the sun. The sun was supposed to be born in the morning and to die in the evening ; or, if a longer life was given to him, it was the short life of one year. At the end of that the sun died, as we still say, the old year dies.
Immortality.

But by the side of this conception, another would spring up. As light and life come from the east, the east, among many of the nations of antiquity, was looked upon as the abode of the bright gods, the eternal home of the immortals ; and when the idea had once arisen that the departed or blessed among men joined the company of the gods, then they also might be transferred to the east.

In some such sense we see that Aditi is called ' the birthplace of the immortals ; ' and in a similar sense one of the Vedic poets sings2: 'Who will give us back to the great Aditi ; that I may see father and mother 1 ' Is not this a beautiful intimation of immortality, simple and perfectly natural ; and if you look back to the steps which led to it, suggested by the ordinary events of everyday life, interpreted by the unassisted wisdom of the human heart ?

Here is the great lesson which the Veda teaches us! All our thoughts, even the apparently most abstract, have their natural beginnings in what 1 H. Spencer, 'Sociology/ I, p. 221. 2 Rig- Veda, I, 24, i.

THE IDEAS OF INFINITY AND LAW. 233 passes daily before our senses. Niliil in fide nisi quod ante fuerit in sensu. Man may for a time be unheedful of these voices of nature ; but they come again and again, day after day, night after night, till at last they are heeded. And if once heeded, those voices disclose their purport more and more clearly, and what seemed at first a mere sunrise, becomes in the end a visible revelation of the infinite, while the setting of the sun is trans figured into the first vision of immortality.
Other religious ideas in the Veda.

Let us examine one more of those ideas which to us seem too abstract and too artificial to be ascribed to a very early stratum of human thought, but which, if we may judge from the Veda, had risen in the human heart at the very first burst of its intellectual springtide. I do not mean to make the Veda more primitive than it is. I know full well the interminable vista of its antecedents. There is ring within ring in the old tree, till we can count no longer, and are lost in amazement at the long, slow growth of human thought. But by the side of much that sounds recent, there is much that sounds ancient and primitive. And here we ought, I think, to learn a lesson from archseology, and not try to lay down from the beginning a succession of sharply divided periods of thought. For a long time archasologists taught that there was first a period of stone, during which no weapons, no tools of bronze or iron, could possibly occur. That period was supposed to be followed by the bronze period, where the graves might yield both bronze and stone implements in abundance, but not a single trace of iron. Lastly, 234 LECTURE V.
we were told, came the third period, clearly marked by the prevalence of iron instruments, which, when they had once been introduced, soon superseded both stone and bronze workmanship altogether.

This theory of the three periods, with their smaller subdivisions, contained no doubt some truth, but being accepted as a kind of archseological dogma, it impeded for a long time, like all dogma, the progress of independent observation ; till at last it was discovered that much in the successive or contemporaneous use of the metals depended on local conditions, and that where mineral or palnstric or meteoric iron existed in an easily accessible form, iron implements might be found and were found together with stone weapons, and previous to bronze workmanship.

This ought to be a warning to us against our preconceived theories as to the succession of intel lectual periods. There are in the Veda thoughts as rude and crude as any paleolithic weapons, but by the side of them, we find thoughts with all the sharpness of iron and all the brilliancy of bronze. Are we to say that the bright and bril liant thoughts must be more modern than the rude ly chipped flints that lie by their side? They may be, but let us remember who the workman is, and that there has been genius at all times, and that genius is not bound by years. To a man who has faith in himself and in the world around him, one glance is as good as a thousand obser vations ; to a true philosopher, the phenomena of nature, the names given to them, the gods who represent them, all vanish by one thought like the mist of the morning, and he declares in the poetical THE IDEAS OF INFINITY AND LAW. 235 language of the Veda, ' There is but One, though the poets call it by many names/ JEkam sat viprd bahudhd vadanti.

No doubt, we may say, the many names of the poets must have come first, before the philosophers could discard them. True, but the poets may have continued for ages invoking Indra, Mitra, Varuna, or Agni, while at the same time the philosophers of India protested, as Herakleitos protested and pro tested in vain, against the many names and the many temples and the many legends of the gods.
The idea of law.
zh: 我相信，阿底提（Aditi）——即「無邊者」——是黎明最古老的名稱之一，或者更精確地說，是指每日清晨世界之光與生命從中迸發的那片天域，這一點幾乎毋庸置疑。

請看那黎明，暫且忘卻你的天文學；我要問你，當黑夜的暗幕緩緩升起，空氣變得透明而充滿生機，光芒四射而出，你不知其從何而來——難道你不會覺得，你的眼睛盡其所能地伸展，卻仍徒勞無功，彷彿正凝視著無限者本身的眼眸嗎¹？對古代的觀者而言，黎明彷彿開啟了通往另一個世界的金色大門，而當這些大門為太陽的凱旋行進而敞開時，他們的雙眼與心智，便以那種稚拙的方式，竭力想要穿透這個有限世界的界線。黎明來了又去，但在黎明背後，始終存在著那片她從中湧現的、起伏不定的光之海或火之海。這難道不就是那可見的無限者嗎？又有什麼名稱，能比吠陀詩人賦予它的稱呼——阿底提（Aditi），即無邊際者、彼方者、超越一切萬有者——更為恰當呢？¹ 因此，我相信我們能夠理解，一位最初在我們看來如此抽象、以至於在自然界中似乎無處可尋其根源，如此現代、以至於我們幾乎難以相信它會出現在《吠陀》中的神祇，何以可能正是印度心靈最早期的直觀與創造之一¹。在後世，無邊際的阿底提或許被等同於天空，也等同於大地，但最初，她遠遠超越於天空與大地之上。

我們在梨俱吠陀中讀到一首頌歌（hymn），是獻給密多羅（Mitra）與伐樓那（Varuṇa）——晝與夜的代表——其中寫道：「哦，密多羅啊！」我已在《梨俱吠陀》的翻譯中，於《梨俱吠陀本集》第一卷（第230–251頁）詳細論述了阿底提（Aditi）。阿爾弗雷德‧希勒布蘭特博士（Dr. Alfred Hillebrandt）曾撰寫一篇傑出的論文〈論阿底提女神〉（Über die Göttin Aditi, 1876）。他（第11頁）將該詞溯源於字根 *da*，意為「束縛」，但更傾向於將阿底提解釋為「不朽性」（imperishableness），並反對將阿底提理解為「無所不在」的觀點。
以及瓦爾特瓦（Vartwa），你登上你的戰車，這戰車在黎明破曉時呈金色，而在日落時分則有鐵製的車轅；從那裡你看見阿底提（Aditi）與底提（Diti）¹——亦即，彼處與此處、無限者與有限者、有死之物與不朽之物²。
另一位詩人將黎明描述為阿底提（Aditi）的面容³，從而表明此處的阿底提並非黎明本身，而是超越黎明的某種存在。

正如太陽與所有太陽神祇自東方升起，我們不難理解何以阿底提（Aditi）被稱為光明諸神之母，尤其指密陀羅（Mitra）與伐樓那（Varuṇa）（《梨俱吠陀》X, 36, 3）、阿利耶門（Aryaman）與跋伽（Bhaga），最終更成為七位乃至八位所謂阿底提耶（Ādityas）——即自東方升起的太陽神祇——之母。蘇利耶（Sūrya），即太陽，不僅被稱為阿底提耶（Āditya）（《梨俱吠陀》VIII, 101, 11：*baḍ mahān asi sūrya, baḍ āditya mahān asi*，「誠然，蘇利耶，汝為偉大；誠然，阿底提耶，汝為偉大」），亦被稱為阿底提耶（Āditeya）（《梨俱吠陀》X, 88, 11）。
無疑地，正是由於頻繁提及這些兒子，才使阿底提（Aditi）幾乎從一開始便具有明確的女性特質。她是母親，擁有強大、可畏且高貴的兒子。然而，有些段落中，阿底提似乎被設想為男性神祇，或至少是無性別的存在。
雖然阿底提（Aditi）與黎明關係更為密切，但她不僅在清晨，同樣也在正午與傍晚受到祈請 4。
1 晨光與暮光的對比，似乎透過金與鐵這兩種金屬的色澤來表現。 2 梨俱吠陀，I, 35, 2。
3 同上，I, 113, 19，dditer anikam。 4 同上，V, 69, 3。
當我們在《阿闥婆吠陀》X, 8, 16 中讀到：「太陽從那兒升起，又在那兒落下，我相信那是最古老的，無人能超越其外」，我們幾乎可以將「最古老的」譯為阿底提（Aditi）。阿底提很快便獲得完全的崇敬與禮拜，人們不僅祈求她驅散黑暗與潛伏於黑暗中的仇敵，同樣也祈求她將人從可能犯下的任何罪孽中解救出來。

然而，我們必須追問：這種對「無限者」的感知，是否可能源自任何其他根源，而非感官所及的那些有限事物？對此，我的回答是：不可能。人類心靈中若無某種對「無限者」的感知，則宗教便不可能誕生；然而，這種感知若要被喚醒，則必須透過某種有限之物。正如眼睛需要光才能看見，耳朵需要聲音才能聽見，同樣地，心靈中對無限者的感知，也需要某種有限之物作為觸媒，才能被激發而進入意識。這有限之物可能是天空、是太陽、是風、是雷電，也可能是河流、是山嶽、是大地，甚至是人類自身。但無論如何，若無有限者，無限者便永遠沉睡於心靈深處，如同種子蟄伏於土壤，等待陽光與雨露的召喚。 因此，宗教的起源，並非始於對無限者的直接直觀，而是始於對有限者的感知——然而，這種感知並非僅僅停留在有限者本身，而是穿透有限者，觸及那隱藏於其後的無限者。這便是宗教感知的獨特之處：它看見有限者，卻不將其視為終極；它觸及有限者，卻在其中發現無限者的蹤跡。正如我們在語言科學中所見，每一個詞語最初都是對某個具體事物的命名，但隨著時間推移，這些詞語逐漸承載了抽象的概念，甚至指向那不可名狀的無限者。同樣地，宗教的詞彙與象徵，最初也源自對自然現象的觀察與敬畏，而後才逐漸昇華為對神聖者的崇拜。 然而，這種從有限到無限的躍進，並非總是順利無礙。人類心靈在追尋無限者的過程中，往往會迷失方向，將有限者本身誤認為無限者，從而陷入偶像崇拜或迷信的泥沼。這便是宗教史上的「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）：當人們忘記了詞語只是符號，而將其視為實體本身時，宗教便從對無限者的敬畏墮落為對有限者的崇拜。因此，宗教學的任務，不僅在於追溯宗教的起源，更在於釐清這種從有限到無限的轉化過程，並揭示其中可能出現的扭曲與偏差。 正是在這個意義上，我們可以理解為何吠陀（Veda）中的早期頌歌，雖然充滿了對自然現象的讚美，卻同時蘊含著對更高神聖者的隱約渴望。梨俱吠陀（Rig-Veda）中的詩人，在歌頌黎明女神烏莎斯（Ushas）或火神阿耆尼（Agni）時，並非僅僅在讚美這些自然現象本身，而是在它們身上瞥見了某種超越性的力量。這種力量，有時被稱為「特尤斯」（Dyaus），即天父；有時被稱為「提婆」（Deva），即光明者。這些名稱，最初或許只是對自然現象的描述，但隨著時間的推移，它們逐漸承載了對無限者的感知，從而成為宗教崇拜的對象。 然而，這種從有限到無限的躍進，並非一蹴可幾。在宗教的早期階段，我們往往看到一種被稱為「單一神教」（henotheism）或「輪換主神教」（kathenotheism）的現象：即崇拜者在一段時間內只專注於一位神祇，將其視為至高無上的存在，但並不否認其他神祇的存在。這種現象，在吠陀宗教中尤為明顯。例如，當詩人歌頌因陀羅（Indra）時，他會將因陀羅描述為宇宙的主宰；但當他轉而歌頌伐樓那（Varuna）時，伐樓那又成為至高無上的神。這種看似矛盾的現象，實際上反映了人類心靈在追尋無限者過程中的一種嘗試：透過輪換崇拜不同的神祇，來逐步接近那終極的無限者。 因此，宗教的發展，並非一條直線前進的道路，而是一條充滿曲折與試誤的旅程。從多神教（polytheism）到一神教（monotheism），從對自然現象的崇拜到對道德律令的敬畏，人類心靈在不斷地超越自身，試圖觸及那不可觸及的無限者。而宗教學的使命，便是記錄並分析這一旅程，揭示其中的智慧與謬誤，從而幫助我們更清晰地理解人類心靈最深處的渴望與追求。

這兩個觀念——黑暗與罪惡——在我們看來相距甚遠，但在早期雅利安人的心中卻緊密相連。我將為你們朗讀一些摘錄，以說明一個觀念（對敵人的恐懼）如何經常引發另一個觀念（對罪惡的恐懼），或者說，我們所謂的「我們最惡劣的敵人」。* 哦，阿底提耶們¹，求禰們拯救我們脫離狼口，如同拯救被捆綁的竊賊，哦，阿底提²！』『願阿底提在白天保護我們的牛群，願那位永不欺騙者於夜間守護；願她以穩定的增長，保護我們脫離邪惡。』（*Amhas*，字面義為「焦慮」，由罪惡意識所產生的窒息感。）『願智慧的阿底提在白天帶著幫助來到我們身邊！願她仁慈地帶來幸福，並驅逐所有敵人！』
或又如³：「阿底提、密多羅，以及伐樓拿，請寬恕，若我們曾對你們犯下任何罪過！願我獲得那寬廣無畏的光明，哦，因陀羅！願漫長的黑暗不降臨我們！」「願阿底提賜予我們無罪⁴！」
另一個觀念似乎很自然地……¹ ¹ 梨俱吠陀，VIII，67，14。² 同上，VIII，18，6，7。
從阿底提（Aditi）的概念中萌生。無論我們往何處探尋，都會發現：對來世最古老的想像之一，源於人們對太陽及其他天體每日出沒的沉思¹。正如我們至今仍說「他的太陽已西沉」，古人同樣認為，離世者將前往西方，即日落之處。太陽被視為晨生暮死；即便賦予它更長的生命，也不過是短短一年的壽命。年終之際，太陽便告消亡——正如我們至今所說：舊歲已逝。

> “不朽，在宗教學中，並非僅僅指靈魂在死後繼續存在；它更常指涉一種超越時間與變化的生命狀態，是與無限者（the Infinite）合一後的永恆歸宿。在吠陀（Veda）與奧義書（Upanishad）的傳統中，這種不朽並非由外在賦予，而是透過認識自我（Ātman）與梵（Brahman）的同一性而證得。穆勒（Müller）指出，雅利安（Aryan）民族的早期祈禱中，對不朽的渴望往往與對光明、太陽及天父特尤斯（Dyaus）的崇拜交織，而這種渴望在後來的輪換主神教（kathenotheism）與單一神教（henotheism）階段，逐漸凝聚為對一神教（monotheism）的信仰基礎。相較之下，閃語族（Semitic）傳統則更強調神聖意志的絕對性，不朽被視為順服於唯一真神後的恩賜。穆勒認為，若將不朽僅視為「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）所導致的隱喻誤解，便會忽略它在比較神學（comparative theology）與比較神話學（comparative mythology）中作為核心象徵的深層意義。”
但在這種觀念之旁，另一種觀念亦將應運而生。由於光明與生命源自東方，在許多古代民族眼中，東方被視為光明諸神的居所、不朽者的永恆家園；而一旦萌生「逝者或蒙福之人加入諸神行列」的想法，他們也便可能被遷移至東方。
在某種意義上，我們看到阿底提被稱為「不朽者的誕生之地」；而一位吠陀詩人亦以類似意涵吟唱道：「誰能將我們歸還給偉大的阿底提，好讓我得見父親與母親？」¹ 這豈非對不朽之美的暗示，既單純又全然自然？若你回顧引向此一領悟的歷程——由日常生活的平凡事件所啟發，並經由人類心靈未經輔助的智慧所詮釋——豈不更是如此？
吠陀教導我們的偉大教訓在此！我們所有的思想，即便是表面上最抽象的，其自然起源皆始於……¹ ¹ H. Spencer，《社會學》卷一，第221頁。² 梨俱吠陀，I, 24, i。
233 每日在我們感官前流逝。*Nihil in fide nisi quod ante fuerit in sensu*（信仰中若無先前感官經驗，便一無所有）。人或許會暫時對這些自然的聲音不加理會；但它們一再降臨，日復一日，夜復一夜，直到最終被留意。而一旦被留意，這些聲音便愈發清晰地揭示其意涵；起初看似僅為日出之物，最終成為無限者可見的啟示，而日落則轉化為不朽的初見。

在梨俱吠陀（Rig-Veda）的讚歌中，我們不僅發現了對自然現象的崇拜，也發現了對抽象力量的祈求。例如，有對「信仰」（Śraddhā）的讚歌，將之視為一種神聖的原則；也有對「憤怒」（Manyu）的讚歌，將其擬人化為一種驅動力。這些觀念顯示，吠陀詩人已開始從具體的自然力量，轉向更為內在或精神性的力量。 此外，我們也觀察到一種被稱為「單一神教」（henotheism）或「輪換主神教」（kathenotheism）的獨特現象。在特定的讚歌中，每一位被呼喚的神祇，如因陀羅（Indra）、阿耆尼（Agni）或伐樓那（Varuna），都被暫時視為至高無上的存在，彷彿祂就是唯一的神。這種對單一神祇的專注崇拜，並非否認其他神祇的存在，而是在崇拜的當下，將該神祇提升至獨尊的地位。這與後來發展出的嚴格「一神教」（monotheism）有所不同，也與明確的「多神教」（polytheism）有別。 穆勒（Müller）認為，這種宗教思維的發展，與「語言科學」（the science of language）所揭示的現象密切相關。他著名的「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）理論，便試圖解釋神話的起源：當用來描述自然現象的原始詞彙，其原本的隱喻意義被遺忘時，便會產生神話。例如，「特尤斯」（Dyaus）原意為「閃耀的天空」，後來被誤解為一個獨立的神祇「天父」，而「提婆」（Deva）則泛指「發光者」或「神靈」。透過比較神話學（comparative mythology），我們得以追溯這些觀念在雅利安（Aryan）語族中的演變，並與閃語族（Semitic）及圖蘭語族（Turanian）的宗教觀念進行比較，從而建立一門真正的「宗教學」（the science of religion）。

讓我們再審視一個觀念，這個觀念對我們而言似乎過於抽象、過於人為，以致無法歸屬於人類思想極早期的層次；然而，若我們能從《吠陀》來判斷，它卻在人類理智春潮初次湧現之際，便已從人類心靈中升起。我並非意圖將《吠陀》說得比它實際更為原始。我深知其前身那無盡的景深。古樹之中有層層年輪，直到我們再也數不清，並因人類思想漫長而緩慢的生長而驚嘆失神。但在許多聽來新近的內容旁邊，也有許多聽來古老而原始的東西。我認為，我們應當從考古學中學到教訓，不要從一開始就試圖劃定一系列截然分明的思想時期。長久以來，考古學家教導我們：首先是一個石器時期，在此期間不可能出現任何青銅或鐵製的武器或工具。這個時期之後據信是青銅時期，此時的墓葬可能大量出土青銅與石器，卻沒有一絲鐵器的痕跡。最後，
我們被告知，接著進入了第三時期，其明確特徵是鐵製器具的盛行；這些器具一旦被引入，便迅速完全取代了石器與青銅器工藝。

這應是對我們的一項警示，提醒我們不要固守那些關於思想時期先後順序的預設理論。在《吠陀》中，既有如舊石器時代武器般粗陋原始的思想，但在它們旁邊，我們也發現了具有鐵器般鋒利與青銅般璀璨的思想。難道我們要說，那些明亮而輝煌的思想，必定比旁邊那些粗糙打製的燧石更為現代嗎？它們或許如此，但讓我們記住那位工匠是誰，並且明白天才在任何時代都存在，而天才不受歲月的束縛。對於一個對自己與周遭世界懷有信心的人而言，一瞥之見勝過千次觀察；對一位真正的哲學家來說，自然的現象、賦予它們的名稱、以及代表它們的神祇，全都因一個念頭而消散，如同晨霧一般；於是他以《吠陀》中詩意的語言宣告：「實在唯一，詩人雖以多名稱之。」（*Ekam sat viprā bahudhā vadanti*）
無疑地，我們可以說，詩人們的眾多名稱必定先於哲學家們能夠摒棄它們而存在。誠然，詩人們可能持續數個世紀呼喚因陀羅（Indra）、密多羅（Mitra）、伐樓那（Varuna）或阿耆尼（Agni），與此同時，印度的哲學家們卻如同赫拉克利特（Herakleitos）徒勞地抗議再抗議一般，反對眾神那繁多的名號、眾多的廟宇與無數的傳說。

當我們說宗教學（the science of religion）必須奠基於比較神學（comparative theology）與比較神話學（comparative mythology）時，這並非僅是方法論上的偏好，而是基於一項根本的認知：正如語言科學（the science of language）透過比較不同語系——雅利安（Aryan）、閃語族（Semitic）、圖蘭語族（Turanian）——的結構，才得以揭示語言的內在法則；同樣地，唯有透過對各宗教傳統——從吠陀（Veda）與梨俱吠陀（Rig-Veda）中的單一神教（henotheism）與輪換主神教（kathenotheism），到奧義書（Upanishad）對無限者（the Infinite）的追尋，乃至希臘人對特尤斯（Dyaus）與提婆（Deva）的崇拜——進行系統性的比較，我們才能辨識出宗教現象背後的法則。穆勒（Müller）曾指出，若無此比較基礎，我們便可能將多神教（polytheism）與一神教（monotheism）之間的過渡視為偶然，或將神話中的矛盾歸咎於「語言的疾病」（a disease of language），而無法理解這些現象實則遵循著某種深層的秩序。
